L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Brett was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and described as 'desperate' to get one final jump before his flight home the next morning. This self-imposed time pressure and urgency overrode his assessment of clearly dangerous conditions, the decision of two companions to walk down, and an explicit warning that it was not worth it. The desperation to capitalize on the last available opportunity drove the decision to launch in winds that were beyond safe limits.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was rushing through packing the evening before in order to go to sleep early and catch early morning conditions. This haste degraded the care and attention applied to the packing process, directly contributing to the bridle being misrouted around the right leg strap without detection.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his companion performed a gear check before exit. The narrative explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle routing would have immediately revealed the misrouting. This omitted preparatory step allowed the packing error from the previous evening to persist undetected through gearing up and exit, enabling the total malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper overestimated his ability to maintain altitude awareness during proximity wingsuit flight in complete flat light. His relaxed posture and absence of urgency throughout the flight indicate he was confident in his altitude perception despite conditions that predictably eliminated all visual depth cues required for terrain-proximity flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was on a dedicated expedition to Baffin Island to fly new wingsuit lines, representing significant investment in travel, logistics, and limited weather windows in a remote Arctic location. This expedition context plausibly contributed to the decision to proceed in flat-light conditions rather than waiting for adequate visibility, consistent with reluctance to forgo opportunities after major investment of effort.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture created complete flat light that caused the jumper's visual system to fail to correctly perceive his altitude and distance relative to the snowfield. The domain reference explicitly identifies flat light and snow cover as conditions producing spatial disorientation. The helmet camera confirms zero corrective input, consistent with a total failure of altitude perception.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate line familiarity
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper planned to fly new (previously unflown) wingsuit proximity lines, contrary to accepted practice requiring intimate knowledge of the entire line before flying it and maintaining distance on first flights. Flying unfamiliar lines compounded the risk of the flat-light decision by removing any mental reference points from prior flights that might have supplemented degraded visual depth cues.

L2_preconditions.label: Flat light from featureless snow cover
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow eliminating all shadows, contrast, and texture on the snowfield, combined with low-level clouds, actively degraded the jumper's visual perception during flight execution. These environmental conditions directly caused the altitude misperception coded at 103O by removing every available depth cue from the terrain surface.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps and 3 seasons, the jumper assessed himself as capable of jumping in 20-knot sustained crosswinds with gusts well over 30 knots—conditions that four experienced companions independently evaluated as unsafe and declined. His self-assessment was clearly inconsistent with both the demands of the conditions and the consensus judgment of the group, indicating he unreasonably overestimated his capability relative to the specific task.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: After spending the entire morning at the exit watching conditions and watching all four companions stand down, the jumper chose to stay and wait another hour rather than walk away. His reluctance to abort after investing significant time and effort at the exit point, and his determination to jump despite unchanged dangerous conditions, indicates motivation driven by factors other than a sound assessment of readiness and conditions—consistent with sunk-cost commitment to the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: During the 3–4 seconds of tumbling along the cliff face, the jumper focused all conscious attention on recovering a stable body position to the exclusion of the higher-priority task of deploying his parachute from any attitude. This fixation on stability recovery prevented him from ever reaching his pilot chute, despite the accepted priority order requiring immediate deployment regardless of body position.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient emergency deployment training
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps over 3 seasons, the jumper lacked the ingrained muscle memory to immediately initiate deployment from a tumbling, head-low attitude near the cliff face. The accepted practice of deploying from any freefall attitude ('whatever happens, pull') requires trained automatic responses developed through extensive practice; his failure to prioritize deployment over stability recovery during the available 3–4 seconds suggests this critical emergency response was not sufficiently developed.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption the night before—vomiting twice on the approach, splitting headache, visibly unsteady. This hangover state degraded his judgment capacity, contributing to his decision to jump despite obvious signs of incapacity and explicit warnings from companions.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states 'the hangover affected his coordination,' which caused his legs to buckle on the exit push, producing the immediate instability that triggered the fixation on recovery. The degraded cognitive function from severe hangover also reduced his capacity to manage the emergency correctly by prioritizing deployment over stability recovery.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states Chris had jumped hung over before without issues, leading him to overestimate his ability to perform while severely impaired. His statement that he would 'be fine once the adrenaline kicked in' demonstrates a specific misjudgment of his capability relative to the demands of this jump, despite his condition being visibly worse than companions had seen before.

L2_preconditions.label: Normalization of deviance
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states 'Chris had jumped hung over before without issues' and 'the decision to jump was not unusual for him,' indicating a sustained pattern of jumping while impaired that he had gradually accepted as his personal norm. This pattern went uncorrected despite being a known risk, removing the internal check that might have prevented the fatal decision to jump in a severely degraded state.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper unreasonably overestimated his ability to fly a tracking suit he had completed only 2 skydives in, basing his self-assessment on watching video footage rather than personal proficiency. He stated the intermediate-to-advanced exit 'looked easy from the footage' and rejected explicit advice from a more experienced jumper to select a more forgiving exit. His self-assessment was grossly inconsistent with his actual capability, directly enabling the decision to attempt the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit point. Acute sleep deprivation from overnight driving degrades judgment and decision-making capacity. This fatigued state impaired his ability to accurately assess risk and contributed to his decision to proceed despite clear indicators that the jump exceeded his capability, including rejecting sound advice from an experienced companion. The exhaustion also likely degraded the physical coordination needed to fly the tracking suit effectively.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit experience for BASE
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had completed only 2 skydives in the tracking suit versus the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before any BASE use. He had never jumped the suit from a cliff. Per accepted progression norms, he had not found the ideal body position for maximum forward speed and glide, did not have consistent openings in the suit, and could not transform an unstable position into a solid track. This directly caused his inability to generate forward drive after exit.

L2_preconditions.label: No slider-up cliff or tracking experience
L2_preconditions.description: All 40 of the jumper's BASE jumps were from lower exits. He had no experience with slider-up cliff objects requiring mandatory sub-terminal tracking. The exit required tracking past a technical section where the wall comes back in — a skill set developed through progressive cliff experience and sub-terminal tracking training. His complete lack of cliff tracking experience meant he had no foundation for the performance demands of this jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient emergency off-heading correction training
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps, the jumper had not internalized the correct emergency procedure for a static line 180° off-heading pointed directly at the object. The domain reference explicitly prescribes rear risers for this scenario, yet she defaulted to toggles without hesitation, indicating this specific emergency response had not been trained to the point of automatic recall. The domain reference emphasizes that off-heading corrections must be practiced extensively at simple spans until reactions become natural and instant.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient emergency off-heading correction training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's inability to locate both toggles and execute a coordinated brake correction indicates insufficient muscle memory for emergency heading corrections. With 90 BASE jumps, she had not developed the practiced, automatic fine-motor skill needed to reliably grab both toggles under time pressure. The domain reference notes that toggles should be big, bright, and stiffened for easy grabbing, and that corrections must be practiced until the reaction is natural and instant.

L2_preconditions.label: Task Saturation
L2_preconditions.description: A static line 180° off-heading directly into a building facade provided an extremely compressed time window of approximately 1-3 seconds in which to assess heading, select a procedure, and execute it. The cognitive and motor demands of this emergency exceeded the jumper's processing capacity, contributing to her failure to locate both toggles and resulting in the uncontrolled single-toggle diving turn into the building.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper explicitly stated he had done this exit dozens of times and knew the heading by feel, believing his extensive site familiarity would compensate for zero visibility. This overestimation of his capability to navigate without visual references directly contributed to his decision to exit when all other jumpers recognized conditions were unsafe and chose to hike down. His self-assessment was clearly inconsistent with the actual demands of the task, as no amount of familiarity enables a jumper to detect an unseen protruding ledge in fog.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Once the jumper began adjusting the helmet-mounted GoPro, his attention became fixated on the camera task to the exclusion of terrain proximity, body position, and object separation. This cognitive narrowing is what prevented him from perceiving the approaching ledge or making any corrective input despite it being in clear view for several seconds, directly enabling the L1 attention failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's designated role as camera person on the two-way created a motivation to ensure the filming was successful. This misplaced prioritization of the filming objective over flight safety drove the decision to reach up and adjust the GoPro during active tracking flight near terrain, rather than abandoning the filming task and maintaining tracking discipline.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate pre-jump camera setup
L2_preconditions.description: Accepted practice requires configuring the camera before exit and forgetting about it during the jump. The fact that the jumper felt compelled to adjust the camera mid-flight indicates the camera was not adequately secured or verified before exit. Proper pre-jump camera preparation would have eliminated the perceived need for an in-flight adjustment, removing the trigger for the L1 decision error.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls from the same AoA-control failure, explicitly identified the corrective action needed (approximately 50 dedicated stall-recovery skydives), communicated this assessment to peers, yet never followed through. He continued jumping cliffs every weekend for three months without addressing the deficiency. This sustained failure to act on a known, self-acknowledged weakness—including normalization of repeated near-misses—enabled the repeated decisions to expose himself to proximity terrain with an unresolved critical skill gap.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: By never returning to the dropzone to practice stall recovery as he himself identified was necessary, the jumper left his AoA-control technique unimproved across three months and multiple additional cliff flights. This sustained inaction on the known technical deficiency directly preserved the conditions under which the fatal stall occurred.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's motivation to continue cliff jumping every weekend was disconnected from his own readiness assessment. He explicitly acknowledged he was not ready for further cliff flights without additional training, yet the desire to keep jumping overrode this self-assessment. His continued participation was driven by the pull of the activity rather than a sound evaluation of his preparedness.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate AoA control skill for proximity flight
L2_preconditions.description: Despite meeting general progression minimums (900 skydives, 300 WS skydives, 150 BASE jumps, 80 WS BASE jumps), the jumper's specific angle-of-attack management skill was demonstrably inadequate for proximity wingsuit flight near terrain. Four prior near-miss stalls from the identical failure pattern confirmed a persistent technique deficit, and the fatal stall at 15 seconds into flight was the same unresolved failure mode. His actual capability was insufficient for the demands of the task he attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: With only 60 BASE jumps and 2 prior antenna jumps—both from taller, more forgiving structures with guy wires further from the exit point—the jumper overestimated his capability to safely jump a more demanding antenna at night. His self-assessment was inconsistent with his actual antenna experience and the restricted heading range (~120°) this object demanded.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses confirmed the jumper completely froze upon seeing guy wires directly ahead after the 90-degree off-heading opening. This automatic physiological freeze response—triggered by the sudden, unexpected proximity of the wires—rendered him unable to initiate any corrective riser or toggle input during the approximately two seconds available before impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper failed to adequately evaluate how the closer guy wire proximity on this 900-foot antenna compared to his two previous antenna jumps from taller structures with wires further from the exit. Additionally, accepted practice requires daytime familiarity with an object before attempting a night jump; there is no evidence he had jumped this antenna in daylight, meaning he lacked knowledge of exit-to-wire distances and heading constraints.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient antenna experience and unpracticed heading corrections
L2_preconditions.description: With only 60 BASE jumps, 2 antenna jumps from more forgiving structures, and no evidence of extensively drilled off-heading correction practice, the jumper lacked the muscle memory required for instant riser or brake input on a restricted-heading object. Accepted practice requires developing heading correction skills through repeated practice at simple spans before progressing to objects where off-headings are immediately life-threatening. This training deficit made the freeze response far more likely and more consequential.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper unreasonably overestimated the capability of his damaged equipment. After examining a ~4-inch tear in the PC mesh, being explicitly told by at least one person that it needed replacement, and being offered spare PCs by multiple jumpers, he concluded it would 'still catch enough air.' This misjudgment of equipment capability directly enabled the decision to jump with critically damaged gear.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate briefing in group jump
L2_preconditions.description: The group failed to establish specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes for the 3-way jump. Without a pre-established separation framework, David had no mental model of where the second jumper would be during tracking and deployment, directly contributing to his failure to check airspace before pitching.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The group's casual approach to the multi-way — simply stating exit order with no further coordination — reflects a complacent attitude toward multi-way procedures that carried into execution. This reduced alertness contributed to David's failure to monitor other jumpers' positions during tracking and to check his airspace before deploying.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: With 120 BASE jumps across 3 seasons, David proceeded with a 3-way cliff jump despite knowing no specific separation plan, delays, or flight paths had been established. This indicates he overestimated the group's collective ability to safely manage a multi-way jump without formal coordination, a task the domain reference identifies as exponentially more risky and requiring thorough pre-planning.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper progressively extended his delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds over the preceding season despite multiple warnings from other jumpers, indicating he unreasonably overestimated his ability to judge minimum safe deployment altitude. Each successful low pull reinforced his belief that he could continue pushing further, creating an inflated self-assessment inconsistent with the actual margins available.

L2_preconditions.label: Normalization of deviance
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was explicitly aware of the increasing risk through multiple direct warnings from other jumpers over the preceding season, yet failed to take corrective action. He gradually accepted progressively lower pulls as his personal standard, with delays escalating from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds, representing a sustained pattern of inaction in response to known feedback about a dangerous trend.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper unreasonably overestimated his ability to handle an expert-level wingsuit in BASE based on his intermediate-suit experience. With only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1—far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 suit-specific skydives—he assessed himself as ready for wingsuit BASE with a suit whose stall boundaries he had not learned. This overestimation of capability directly drove the decision to jump the expert suit off a cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient training in specific wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert wingsuit, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 suit-specific skydives before wingsuit BASE jumping. The domain reference states that even an entry-level suit requires 30-80 BASE jumps to reach full potential, and that upgrading before fully utilizing current suit performance is a recognized error. His insufficient familiarity with the expert suit's narrow margin between efficient flight and deep stall meant he lacked the skill and muscle memory to maintain proper AoA and recover when the suit entered a stall regime, directly producing the fatal technique failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Grossly insufficient skydiving and BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 total skydives, zero BASE jumps, and zero tracking suit experience in skydiving. Accepted practice requires 200+ skydives, an FJC, mentored progression beginning at simple spans, and 40-50 tracking suit skydives before tracking suit BASE use. This extreme experience deficit left the jumper unable to assess the risk of a slider-up cliff jump, directly contributing to the catastrophically poor judgment to attempt the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Grossly insufficient skydiving and BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, the jumper had none of the foundational skills required for cliff jumping: no exit technique, no sub-terminal tracking ability, no body position control at deployment, and no object avoidance proficiency. This total absence of trained skill directly produced the unstable exit and failure to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Attempting a slider-up cliff jump with a tracking suit while possessing only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps indicates the jumper grossly overestimated their capability relative to the demands of the task. The gap between the jumper's actual ability and the minimum competence required for this jump type is so extreme that proceeding reflects an unreasonable self-assessment of capability, contributing directly to the decision to jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: Placing the pilot chute inside the main container instead of routing it to the BOC pouch is a fundamental step omission in a well-practiced routine procedure. With 90 BASE jumps and 500 skydives over three seasons, the jumper had performed this packing sequence many times. The nature of the error—omitting a critical procedural step in a familiar, repetitive task—is characteristic of complacency or inattention during routine performance, where familiarity reduced the alertness needed to catch a gross sequencing mistake.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A pre-jump gear check verifying the deployment system—confirming the pilot chute was properly seated in the BOC pouch and the bridle was correctly routed from PC through the closing pins—would have revealed that the PC was missing from the BOC pouch and trapped inside the container. The error was discovered only by the rescue team after the accident, indicating that no adequate gear check was performed or that the check failed to trace the critical PC-to-BOC routing before exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform adequate pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper exited with misrigged equipment, indicating that the pre-jump gear check was either omitted or insufficiently thorough. A proper gear inspection — tracing the bridle, verifying all connections, slider routing, and line configuration — should have identified the rigging error before jumping. This preparatory omission allowed the L1 misrigging error to persist undetected into the jump.
